title: Stock Position Size Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Position size for stocks: calculate shares to buy based on capital, risk %, and stop-loss distance. Alexander Elder's 2% rule. Updated 2026.
intro: If you're buying shares of Apple, Nvidia, YPF, or GGAL, the first question isn't *how many can I afford* but *how many should I buy to stick to my risk rules*? This calculator applies **Alexander Elder's 2% rule** (Trading for a Living, 1993): never risk more than 2% of your account on a single trade. Enter your capital, entry price, stop-loss level, and risk percentage to find **how many shares to buy**, capital deployed, maximum loss, and the position's percentage of your portfolio. Works for any market: NYSE, NASDAQ, Merval, BYMA, IBEX, or BMV. It's your first filter before hitting enter on the order.
keyTakeaway: **Shares = (Capital × %Risk) / (Entry − Stop)**. Elder's rule: max 2% per trade, max 6% total open.
useCases: Long NVDA with technical stop and 1% risk.
How many GGAL shares to buy risking 2%.
Breakouts with wide stops without overexposing.
Prevent a single position from exceeding 20% of capital.

fields.label: Capital (USD or ARS)
fields.help: Your starting capital. Example: $100,000 in savings.

example.title: 20,000 USD, 1% risk, entry 150 stop 145
example.result: **40 shares**. If stop hits, you lose $200 (1%). Capital deployed: $6,000.

explanation:
## Alexander Elder's 2% Rule

In *Trading for a Living* (1993), Alexander Elder formalized two critical rules:
- **The 2% rule**: never risk more than 2% of your account on a single trade.
- **The 6% rule**: total open risk across all positions ≤ 6%.

Three positions at 2% each bring you to the limit, forcing strict selectivity and discipline.

## The Formula

```
Shares = (Capital × %Risk/100) / |Entry Price − Stop Price|
```

## Risk Table by Capital

| Capital | 0.5% | 1% | 2% |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10,000 | $50 | $100 | $200 |
| 50,000 | $250 | $500 | $1,000 |
| 100,000 | $500 | $1,000 | $2,000 |
| 500,000 | $2,500 | $5,000 | $10,000 |

## Common Mistakes

1. **Deploying all capital**: a 30% drop wipes you out.
2. **Stops too tight** due to low budget: normal volatility will shake you out.
3. **Averaging down**: doubles risk instead of spreading it.
4. **Ignoring commissions** (0.3–0.6% in Argentina, 0–0.1% in the USA).

Complementary tools: [Risk/Reward Ratio](/calculadora-risk-reward-ratio-trade) · [Kelly Criterion](/calculadora-kelly-criterion-apuesta-optima) · [Maximum Drawdown](/calculadora-drawdown-maximo-aceptable)

> ⚠️ Educational calculator only. Not financial advice. Markets carry risk of total loss. Consult a certified advisor before trading.

faq.q: Does this work for US and emerging market stocks?
faq.a: Yes. The formula is market-agnostic. Use the same currency for capital, entry, and stop across all inputs.

faq.q: What if I don't use a stop-loss?
faq.a: Without a stop, there's no rational position sizing. Estimate a mental stop of 10–20% to calculate your shares.
Yes. Add entry and exit commissions to your calculation. Expect ~1% in Argentina, ~0.1% in the USA.

faq.q: Can a single stock be more than 30% of my portfolio?
faq.a: Technically yes if your stop is very tight. Best practice: **no more than 20% per position** to avoid gap risk.

faq.q: Does this work for ADRs or depositary receipts?
faq.a: Yes. Add currency risk (USD/ARS spread) as an additional risk factor in your calculations.
Brokers like IBKR and Robinhood allow fractional shares. If yours doesn't, round down to whole shares.

faq.q: How do I apply Elder's rule across multiple positions?
faq.a: Track open risk on each position. If you have 3 trades at 2% each, you've hit the 6% total limit—no new positions.

faq.q: What's the difference between risk % and portfolio %?
faq.a: Risk % is what you *could lose* if your stop hits. Portfolio % is what *capital* you're deploying. A tight stop means low portfolio but same risk %.

sources.name: Alexander Elder - Trading for a Living (1993)
